title: Sign Certification Requests
text: Sign Certification Requests Certificates need to be signed by an internal or external certificate authority. If your organization acts as its own Certificate Authority, someone must be authorized to sign certification requests from employees that need a certificate for their secure connection. The requestor generates a key for the certificate. For example: openssl genrsa -aes256 -out cert.key 2048 Copy The requestor generates a certificate signing request. For example: openssl req -config openssl.cnf / -key cert.key / -new -sha256 / -out cert.csr Copy The openssl.cnf configuration file is a default configuration file that provides basic information about the certificate owner. The company Certificate Authority signs the request: It is important to know whether the certificate is for a TLS server or a TLS client. In case of a TLS server: openssl ca -config ca_intermediate.cnf / -extensions server_cert / -days 375 -notext -md sha256 / -in cert.csr / -out cert.crt Copy In case of a TLS client: openssl ca -config ca_intermediate.cnf / -extensions usr_cert / -days 375 -notext -md sha256 / -in cert.csr / -out cert.crt Copy The company Certificate Authority verifies the certificate. For example: openssl x509 -noout -text -in cert.crt Copy The certificate is now signed and can be sent back to the requestor. The root CA certificate and the intermediate certificate (not their keys!) must be made available in the trust store or concatenated as a single CA certificate file on the machine that will do the verification.
